title: 85.	Number of ways to stay in the same place after steps (DP).
description: 85.	Number of ways to stay in the same place after steps (DP).

approaches.name: Dynamic Programming with 2D Array O(steps * arrLen)
approaches.summary: Use a 2D DP table where dp[i][j] represents the number of ways to be at position j after i steps, updating based on moves from adjacent positions or staying. This avoids redundant calculations by storing intermediate results.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Ways to Stay in Place After Steps
ladders.desc: Complete solution using 2D DP to count ways to be at position 0 after given steps with movement constraints.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires counting the number of ways to be at position 0 after exactly 'steps' moves, where you can move left, right, or stay in place each step, but cannot move outside array bounds [0, arrLen-1]. The intuition is to model this as a state transition problem: after i steps, the number of ways to be at position j depends on where you could have been after i-1 steps. We use a 2D DP table dp[i][j] where i represents steps taken (0 to steps) and j represents position (0 to min(steps, arrLen)-1 since you can't go further than steps from start). The recurrence is: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] (stay) + dp[i-1][j-1] (move right to j) + dp[i-1][j+1] (move left to j), with boundary checks. Base case: dp[0][0] = 1 (0 steps, at position 0). The answer is dp[steps][0] modulo 10^9+7. Time complexity is O(steps * min(steps, arrLen)) because we only need positions up to min(steps, arrLen) since you can't reach beyond steps from start. Space complexity is the same for the DP table, but can be optimized to O(min(steps, arrLen)) using rolling arrays. Connection to L1: This uses the DP update subroutine to fill the table. Common mistakes include not handling modulo correctly, incorrect boundary conditions, or using full arrLen leading to TLE. Real code pattern: Initialize dp[0][0]=1, loop i from 1 to steps, j from 0 to bound-1, update dp[i][j] with sum of valid previous positions, return dp[steps][0] % MOD.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DP State Update for One Step
ladders.desc: Compute dp[i][j] for all j given dp[i-1] using the recurrence relation.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine updates the DP table for step i based on the previous step i-1. The intuition is that to reach position j after i steps, you must have been at j, j-1, or j+1 after i-1 steps and made the corresponding move (stay, move right, or move left). The algorithm: For each position j from 0 to bound-1 (where bound = min(steps, arrLen)), set dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] (if valid) + dp[i-1][j-1] (if j>0) + dp[i-1][j+1] (if j<bound-1). All operations are modulo 10^9+7. Time complexity for this subroutine is O(bound) per step, and space is O(bound) for storing two rows. Connection to L0: This is the core update called in the main loop. Common mistakes include forgetting modulo, incorrect boundary checks, or using arrLen instead of bound. Real code pattern: new_dp = [0]*bound; for j in range(bound): new_dp[j] = dp_prev[j]; if j>0: new_dp[j] += dp_prev[j-1]; if j<bound-1: new_dp[j] += dp_prev[j+1]; new_dp[j] %= MOD.

examples.trace: Initialize new_dp[3]=[0,0,0]. j=0: new_dp[0]=dp_prev[0]=1 (stay), +dp_prev[1]=1 (move left from 1) =2. j=1: new_dp[1]=dp_prev[1]=1, +dp_prev[0]=1 (move right from 0), +dp_prev[2]=0 (move left from 2) =2, but wait correct: new_dp[1]=dp_prev[1]=1 + dp_prev[0]=1 + dp_prev[2]=0 =2? Actually dp_prev[2]=0, so sum=2. Let's recompute: dp_prev=[1,1,0]. j=0: new_dp[0]=dp_prev[0] + dp_prev[1] =1+1=2. j=1: new_dp[1]=dp_prev[1] + dp_prev[0] + dp_prev[2] =1+1+0=2. j=2: new_dp[2]=dp_prev[2] + dp_prev[1] =0+1=1. Output [2,2,1]. Correction: My earlier trace had error, output is [2,2,1].
new_dp[0]=dp_prev[0]=2 + dp_prev[1]=2 =4. new_dp[1]=dp_prev[1]=2 + dp_prev[0]=2 =4. Output [4,4].

ladders.title: Core Logic — State Transition with Adjacency Moves
ladders.desc: Understand how moves (stay, left, right) translate to DP recurrence.
ladders.explanation: The core logic is the state transition: from a state (position) at step i-1, you can transition to neighboring states at step i by moving left, right, or staying. This is modeled as dp[i][j] = sum of dp[i-1][k] for k in {j-1, j, j+1} where k is valid. The intuition is combinatorial: each way to reach j after i steps is formed by taking a way to reach a neighboring position after i-1 steps and making the appropriate move. This avoids exponential exploration by using DP. Time and space are linear in number of states per step. Connection to L1: This defines the recurrence used in the update subroutine. Common mistakes include missing the 'stay' move or incorrect adjacency. Real code pattern: Conceptually, for each j, collect contributions from j-1, j, j+1 with bounds checking.

examples.input: Previous step counts: at position 0:2 ways, position 1:3 ways. Compute ways to be at position 0 next.
examples.trace: To be at position 0 next step: can stay from position 0 (2 ways) or move left from position 1 (3 ways). Sum = 2+3=5.

examples.input: Previous: pos0:1, pos1:2, pos2:1. Compute ways to be at position 1 next.
examples.trace: To be at position 1: stay from pos1 (2), move right from pos0 (1), move left from pos2 (1). Sum=2+1+1=4.

testCases.input: prev: pos0=1, pos1=1, pos2=1; target j=1
prev: pos0=0, pos1=0, pos2=5; target j=2
prev: pos0=2, pos1=2, pos2=2; target j=1
prev: pos0=1, pos1=0, pos2=0; target j=0
prev: pos0=0, pos1=10, pos2=0; target j=1

ladders.title: Building Block — Bounded 1D Array DP with Base Case
ladders.desc: Initialize and iterate DP over steps with limited positions.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves setting up a DP array for positions limited by bound = min(steps, arrLen), because you cannot move more than steps away from start. Initialize dp[0] = 1 at position 0, others 0, representing 0 steps taken. Then for each step from 1 to steps, compute new_dp from dp using the transition. The intuition is to iteratively build up the solution, ensuring we only consider reachable positions. Time O(steps * bound), space O(bound). Connection to L2: This implements the transition logic in a loop. Common mistakes include incorrect bound calculation or not resetting base case. Real code pattern: dp = [0]*bound; dp[0]=1; for i in range(steps): new_dp = [0]*bound; for j in range(bound): update new_dp[j]; dp = new_dp.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Modular Addition with Bounds Check
ladders.desc: Safely add values from array indices with modulo arithmetic.
ladders.explanation: This operation involves adding values from an array at indices j-1, j, j+1, ensuring indices are within bounds [0, bound-1], and applying modulo 10^9+7 to prevent overflow. The intuition is to handle the combinatorial sums correctly while respecting array limits. Step-by-step: For a given index j, initialize sum=0. If j >=0 and j < bound, add dp[j]. If j-1 >=0, add dp[j-1]. If j+1 < bound, add dp[j+1]. Then sum %= MOD. Time O(1) per addition, space O(1). Connection to L3: This is used in the inner loop to compute new_dp[j]. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors or missing modulo. Real code pattern: val = dp[j]; if j>0: val += dp[j-1]; if j<bound-1: val += dp[j+1]; val %= MOD.

examples.trace: val = dp[1]=2. j>0 true: add dp[0]=1 -> 3. j<2 true: add dp[2]=3 -> 6. 6 % MOD = 6.
val = dp[0]=5. j>0 false. j<1 true: add dp[1]=0 -> 5. 5 % MOD =5.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Indexing and Basic Loops
ladders.desc: Access array elements and iterate with simple loops.
ladders.explanation: This foundation covers basic programming concepts: creating an array of size n, accessing elements by index (0-based), and using for-loops to iterate. The intuition is that arrays allow storing multiple values, and loops enable repetitive operations. Step-by-step: To create an array 'arr' of size n with zeros: arr = [0]*n. To set value at index i: arr[i] = value. To access: x = arr[i]. To loop through indices: for i in range(n): use arr[i]. Time O(n) for looping, space O(n) for array. Connection to L4: This is used to manage the DP array. Common mistakes include index out of range or incorrect loop ranges. Real code pattern: n = 5; arr = [0]*n; arr[0] = 1; for i in range(n): print(arr[i]).

examples.input: Create array size 3, set indices 0 and 2 to 1, loop to print.
Array size 2, set both to 5, sum elements.

testCases.input: size=4, set indices 0 and 3 to 1, count ones
size=5, set index i to i, sum
size=3, set to [6,7,8], increment each by 1

approaches.name: Space-Optimized DP O(steps * arrLen)
approaches.summary: Optimize the 2D DP approach by using only two 1D arrays (or one with careful updating) to track the current and previous step's ways, reducing space usage. This leverages the observation that each step depends only on the previous step.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Space-Optimized DP for Number of Ways to Stay in Place
ladders.desc: Complete solution using space-optimized DP with two 1D arrays to track ways for each position across steps.
ladders.explanation: This problem asks: given arrLen positions (0-indexed) and steps moves, starting at position 0, how many ways to end at position 0 after exactly steps moves? Each move: stay, left (if not at 0), or right (if not at arrLen-1). The space-optimized DP approach reduces memory from O(steps * arrLen) to O(min(steps, arrLen)) by observing each step depends only on the previous step. Intuition: we only need two arrays—prev (ways from previous step) and curr (ways for current step being computed). For each step, for each position i, curr[i] = prev[i] (stay) + (prev[i-1] if i>0) (move from left) + (prev[i+1] if i<arrLen-1) (move from right). Since we can't move beyond min(steps, arrLen) positions from start (as steps limit reach), we cap array size to min(steps, arrLen) + 1 for efficiency. Initialize prev[0]=1 (start at position 0). After steps iterations, answer is prev[0] (or curr[0] after last step). Time: O(steps * min(steps, arrLen))—for each step, iterate over capped positions. Space: O(min(steps, arrLen)) for two arrays. Connection to L1: this uses the sub-routine of updating arrays per step. Common mistakes: not capping array size (wastes space), incorrect boundary checks, forgetting modulo (10^9+7). Real code pattern: initialize dp_prev = [0]*size, dp_prev[0]=1; for _ in range(steps): dp_curr = [0]*size; for i in range(size): dp_curr[i] = dp_prev[i]; if i>0: dp_curr[i] += dp_prev[i-1]; if i<size-1: dp_curr[i] += dp_prev[i+1]; dp_curr[i] %= MOD; dp_prev = dp_curr; return dp_prev[0].

examples.trace: size = min(2,3)+1 = 3. Initialize prev = [1,0,0]. Step 1: curr = [0,0,0]. i=0: curr[0] = prev[0] (stay) = 1. i=1: curr[1] = prev[1] (stay) + prev[0] (from left) = 0+1=1. i=2: curr[2] = prev[2] (stay) + prev[1] (from left) = 0+0=0. prev = [1,1,0]. Step 2: curr = [0,0,0]. i=0: curr[0] = prev[0] + prev[1] (from right) = 1+1=2. i=1: curr[1] = prev[1] + prev[0] (from left) + prev[2] (from right) = 1+1+0=2. i=2: curr[2] = prev[2] + prev[1] (from left) = 0+1=1. prev = [2,2,1]. Return prev[0] = 2? Wait, check: after step 2, ways to be at position 0: from prev[0] stay (1 way) + from prev[1] move left (1 way) = 2, but output should be 4. Let's recompute: Step 1: from start (0), ways: stay at 0 (1 way), move to 1 (1 way). So prev after step 1: [1,1,0] correct. Step 2: from prev[0]=1: stay at 0 (1 way). From prev[1]=1: move left to 0 (1 way), stay at 1 (1 way), move right to 2 (1 way). So curr after step 2: [1+1=2, 1+0+1=2, 0+1=1]. But ways to end at 0 after step 2: from curr[0]=2? That's only 2 ways, but problem says 4. Let's list all sequences for arrLen=3, steps=2: start 0. Step 1: stay 0, step 2: stay 0 (1). Step 1: stay 0, step 2: right to 1 (not 0). Step 1: right to 1, step 2: left to 0 (2). Step 1: right to 1, step 2: stay 1 (not 0). Step 1: right to 1, step 2: right to 2 (not 0). That's only 2 ways? Wait, problem example: arrLen=3, steps=2 should output 4. Let's check: sequences: (stay, stay), (right, left), (left, right) but left not allowed at start. Actually, from 0, can't move left. So only (stay,stay) and (right,left) = 2 ways. But problem says 4. I recall: number of ways to stay in same place after steps? Title might be misleading. Actually, problem 1269. Number of Ways to Stay in the Same Place After Some Steps? Let's assume it's standard: ways to be at index 0 after steps. For arrLen=3, steps=2, output should be 4? Let's recompute carefully. From 0, step 1: can stay (0) or right (1). Step 2: from 0: stay (0) or right (1). From 1: left (0), stay (1), right (2). So after step 2, at 0: from 0 stay (1 way) + from 1 left (1 way) = 2 ways. But if output is 4, maybe arrLen=2? Let's test arrLen=2, steps=2: size=min(2,2)+1=3? Actually arrLen=2 means positions 0,1. size=min(2,2)+1=3? No, size should be arrLen capped: min(steps, arrLen) = 2, so size=3? Wait, positions 0,1,2? arrLen=2 means indices 0 and 1. So size should be min(steps, arrLen) but we need +1 for index range? Let's define size = min(steps, arrLen-1) + 1? Actually, from start 0, max reachable index is min(steps, arrLen-1). So size = min(steps, arrLen-1) + 1. For arrLen=3, steps=2: min(2,2)=2, size=3. That's what I used. But output should be 4? Let's list all sequences for arrLen=3, steps=2 that end at 0: (stay,stay), (right,left). That's 2. Maybe problem is different: number of ways to stay in same place? That might mean ways to be at any position? No, title says stay in same place after steps. I think there's confusion. Let's assume problem is: given steps and arrLen, starting at 0, ways to be at 0 after steps. For arrLen=3, steps=2, output is 2. But to match common problem, I'll use example from known source: for arrLen=3, steps=2, output is 4. Let's derive: actually, from 0, step 1: stay (0), right (1). Step 2: from 0: stay (0), right (1). From 1: left (0), stay (1), right (2). So at end, position 0: from 0 stay (1) + from 1 left (1) = 2. To get 4, maybe steps=3? For arrLen=3, steps=3: compute. Initialize prev=[1,0,0]. Step1: curr=[1,1,0]; prev=[1,1,0]. Step2: curr=[2,2,1]; prev=[2,2,1]. Step3: curr=[4,5,3]; prev=[4,5,3]. Return 4. So for steps=3, output=4. So for example, use arrLen=3, steps=3. I'll correct. For L0 example, use arrLen=3, steps=3 output 4. Trace: size=min(3,3)+1=4? arrLen=3, positions 0,1,2. min(steps, arrLen)=3, size=4? Actually, indices 0,1,2 so size=3. min(3,3)=3, but we need size = min(steps, arrLen-1)+1? Since arrLen=3, arrLen-1=2, min(3,2)=2, size=3. That's correct. So size=3. Initialize prev=[1,0,0]. Step1: i=0: curr[0]=prev[0]=1; i=1: curr[1]=prev[1]+prev[0]=0+1=1; i=2: curr[2]=prev[2]+prev[1]=0+0=0. prev=[1,1,0]. Step2: i=0: curr[0]=prev[0]+prev[1]=1+1=2; i=1: curr[1]=prev[1]+prev[0]+prev[2]=1+1+0=2; i=2: curr[2]=prev[2]+prev[1]=0+1=1. prev=[2,2,1]. Step3: i=0: curr[0]=prev[0]+prev[1]=2+2=4; i=1: curr[1]=prev[1]+prev[0]+prev[2]=2+2+1=5; i=2: curr[2]=prev[2]+prev[1]=1+2=3. prev=[4,5,3]. Return prev[0]=4.

size = min(4,2)+1 = 3? arrLen=2, positions 0,1. min(steps, arrLen)=2, size=3? But indices 0,1 so size=2. Actually, from start 0, max reachable index = min(steps, arrLen-1) = min(4,1)=1. So size=2. Initialize prev=[1,0]. Step1: curr[0]=prev[0]=1; curr[1]=prev[1]+prev[0]=0+1=1. prev=[1,1]. Step2: curr[0]=prev[0]+prev[1]=1+1=2; curr[1]=prev[1]+prev[0]=1+1=2. prev=[2,2]. Step3: curr[0]=prev[0]+prev[1]=2+2=4; curr[1]=prev[1]+prev[0]=2+2=4. prev=[4,4]. Step4: curr[0]=prev[0]+prev[1]=4+4=8; curr[1]=prev[1]+prev[0]=4+4=8. prev=[8,8]. Return prev[0]=8.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DP Array Update for One Step
ladders.desc: Update the DP array for a single step given previous step's ways.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine computes the number of ways to reach each position after one move, given the ways from the previous step. Intuition: for each position i, ways to be at i after current step = ways to stay at i from previous step + ways to move from i-1 (if exists) + ways to move from i+1 (if exists). This captures all possible moves: stay, left move (from i+1 to i), right move (from i-1 to i). The sub-routine takes as input an array prev of length size (capped by min(steps, arrLen)), and outputs array curr of same length. For each i from 0 to size-1: initialize curr[i] = prev[i]; if i>0, add prev[i-1]; if i<size-1, add prev[i+1]; then take modulo MOD=10^9+7. Time: O(size) per step. Space: O(size) for curr array. Connection to L0: this is called steps times in the full solution. Common mistakes: incorrect boundary conditions (e.g., accessing prev[-1] or prev[size]), forgetting modulo, not initializing curr properly. Real code pattern: def update_step(prev, size): curr = [0]*size; for i in range(size): curr[i] = prev[i]; if i>0: curr[i] += prev[i-1]; if i<size-1: curr[i] += prev[i+1]; curr[i] %= MOD; return curr.

examples.trace: Initialize curr = [0,0,0]. i=0: curr[0] = prev[0] = 1; since i=0, no i>0; i<size-1? 0<2 true, add prev[1]=1, so curr[0]=2. i=1: curr[1] = prev[1]=1; i>0 true, add prev[0]=1, so 2; i<size-1? 1<2 true, add prev[2]=0, so curr[1]=2. i=2: curr[2] = prev[2]=0; i>0 true, add prev[1]=1, so 1; i<size-1? 2<2 false, so no add. curr=[2,2,1].
curr = [0,0,0]. i=0: curr[0]=prev[0]=2; add prev[1]=2, total 4. i=1: curr[1]=prev[1]=2; add prev[0]=2 and prev[2]=1, total 5. i=2: curr[2]=prev[2]=1; add prev[1]=2, total 3. So [4,5,3].

ladders.title: Core Logic — State Transition for DP
ladders.desc: The recurrence relation that defines ways to reach a position based on previous positions.
ladders.explanation: The core logic is the state transition equation: dp[step][i] = dp[step-1][i] + dp[step-1][i-1] (if i>0) + dp[step-1][i+1] (if i<arrLen-1). This equation is derived from the problem constraints: at each step, you can stay, move left, or move right. Therefore, to be at position i after step, you could have been at i after step-1 and stayed, or at i-1 after step-1 and moved right, or at i+1 after step-1 and moved left. The modulo 10^9+7 is applied to prevent overflow. Intuition: this is a classic DP for 1D random walk with boundaries. The dependency only on previous step allows space optimization. Time per transition is O(1). Connection to L1: this equation is implemented in the update_step sub-routine. Common mistakes: incorrect indices (off-by-one), missing boundary checks, not applying modulo. Real code pattern: for each i, ways = dp_prev[i]; if i>0: ways += dp_prev[i-1]; if i<size-1: ways += dp_prev[i+1]; dp_curr[i] = ways % MOD.

examples.trace: For i=1: start with ways = dp_prev[1] = 1. Since i>0, add dp_prev[0]=1, ways=2. Since i<size-1 (1<2), add dp_prev[2]=0, ways=2. So dp_curr[1] = 2.
For i=0: ways = dp_prev[0]=2. i>0 false. i<size-1 (0<2) true, add dp_prev[1]=2, ways=4. So dp_curr[0]=4.

ladders.title: Building Block — Capping Array Size Based on Steps
ladders.desc: Determine the effective array size to optimize space, based on steps and arrLen.
ladders.explanation: This building block computes the size of the DP array as min(steps, arrLen) + 1? Actually, since we start at index 0 and can move at most steps away, the maximum reachable index is min(steps, arrLen-1). Therefore, the array size needed is min(steps, arrLen-1) + 1. But for simplicity in implementation, often size = min(steps, arrLen) is used, but careful with indices. Intuition: if arrLen is large, we can't reach beyond steps moves from start, so we only need to track positions 0 to steps. If arrLen is small, we are limited by arrLen-1. This capping reduces space from O(arrLen) to O(min(steps, arrLen)). Time: O(1) to compute. Connection to L2: this size is used in the DP arrays. Common mistakes: using arrLen directly (wastes space), incorrect off-by-one in size calculation. Real code pattern: size = min(steps, arrLen-1) + 1.

examples.trace: arrLen-1 = 4, min(steps, arrLen-1) = min(3,4) = 3, size = 3+1 = 4. So positions to track: 0,1,2,3.
arrLen-1 = 1, min(steps, arrLen-1) = min(10,1) = 1, size = 1+1 = 2. So positions: 0,1.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Modulo Arithmetic for Large Numbers
ladders.desc: Apply modulo 10^9+7 to prevent integer overflow in DP calculations.
ladders.explanation: In DP, ways can grow exponentially, so we take modulo 1,000,000,007 (a large prime) at each step to keep numbers manageable and prevent overflow. This operation: x % MOD, where MOD = 10**9+7. Intuition: since the final answer is required modulo MOD, we can apply modulo at each addition without affecting the result, due to properties of modular arithmetic: (a+b) % MOD = (a%MOD + b%MOD) % MOD. This ensures intermediate values stay within integer limits. Time: O(1) per operation. Connection to L3: used after each addition in DP updates. Common mistakes: applying modulo only at the end (risk of overflow), using wrong MOD value. Real code pattern: MOD = 10**9+7; after each addition, value %= MOD.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Initialization and Indexing
ladders.desc: Initialize an array and access elements with boundary checks.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept involves creating an array of a given size, initializing its elements (e.g., to 0), and accessing elements by index while ensuring the index is within bounds [0, size-1]. Intuition: arrays are zero-indexed; accessing out-of-bounds indices causes errors. In DP, we often initialize an array with zeros and set a starting value (e.g., dp[0]=1). Boundary checks are crucial when accessing adjacent indices (i-1, i+1) to avoid index errors. Time: O(size) for initialization, O(1) for access. Space: O(size). Connection to L4: arrays are used to store DP values, and modulo is applied after access. Common mistakes: off-by-one in size, forgetting to initialize, accessing negative indices. Real code pattern: size = 5; arr = [0]*size; arr[0] = 1; for i in range(size): value = arr[i]; if i>0: left = arr[i-1]; if i<size-1: right = arr[i+1].

examples.input: size = 3, set index 0 to 1, access indices 0,1,2
examples.trace: Initialize arr = [0,0,0]. Set arr[0] = 1. Access arr[0] gives 1, arr[1] gives 0, arr[2] gives 0.

examples.input: size = 2, set index 1 to 2, access with boundary checks at i=0
examples.output: arr = [0, 2], at i=0: no left, right=2
examples.trace: arr = [0,0]. Set arr[1]=2. For i=0: value=arr[0]=0; i>0 false so no left; i<size-1? 0<1 true, so right=arr[1]=2.

testCases.input: size = 2, set index 0 to 1, index 1 to 2
size = 5, set index 0 to 1, index 4 to 1
size = 2, access index 0 with boundary check
size = 3, access index 1 with boundary check
size = 1, access index 0 with boundary check
